text: The life of an international organization (IO) does not begin and end with the negotiation and entry into force of the constitution that establishes it. How and why do IOs change over time – in response to changes in the global political economy or in their own internal politics? This special issue is dedicated to the dynamics of international cooperation as exemplified by the lives of the very IOs that comprise the international system. Much of the theoretical work on organizations from the firm theory and organizational sociology traditions and from agency theory has moved in the direction of integrating life cycles into the analysis of organizational behaviour. But the international relations (IR) literature has hardly begun to draw on this rich set of perspectives. Adding the life cycles perspective to the study of IOs involves two crucial elements. The first is that of treating the IO itself rather than its members as the main focus of the analysis; the second is that of examining the dynamics over time of the very organizations themselves as they move through a range of life cycle stages such as formation – or false start –, consolidation – or stagnation –, growth, rejuvenation – or revitalization –, and, in the final stages, death and succession. Incorporating the dynamics of the lives of IOs into our understanding of the international system tells us nothing less than that the history of modern international relations has always been one of competition and struggle marked by periodic periods of stability and flourishing as well as by periods of dysfunction and stagnation. Understanding how and why these different dynamics occur is crucial if we are to want to explain and ensure the stability of the international system. The articles in this Special Issue explore a variety of these themes such as the durability of international organizations during periods of crisis, international organizations’ strategies to achieve legitimacy, leaders’ discursive practices, the balancing act faced by governments prior to the dissolution of an IO (by choosing to keep it alive or by replacing it with a different institution), the systemic impact of withdrawals from an IO made by one or more members, and finally citizens’ attitudes towards such withdrawals.
